# 06. Applying RBAC -- From Go Markers to Auto-Generated YAML

## Core Workflow

```text
Marker comments in Go code     make manifests       config/rbac/ YAML files
─────────────────────────   ──────────────-->   ──────────────────────────
+kubebuilder:rbac:...                           role.yaml (auto-generated)
```

kubebuilder reads the `+kubebuilder:rbac` comments in Go code and auto-generates the RBAC YAML.
There is no need to edit YAML files manually.

## Our Operator's Marker Comments

`internal/controller/simpleapp_controller.go`, lines 40-43:

```go
// +kubebuilder:rbac:groups=apps.example.com,resources=simpleapps,verbs=get;list;watch;create;update;patch;delete
// +kubebuilder:rbac:groups=apps.example.com,resources=simpleapps/status,verbs=get;update;patch
// +kubebuilder:rbac:groups=apps.example.com,resources=simpleapps/finalizers,verbs=update
// +kubebuilder:rbac:groups=apps,resources=deployments,verbs=get;list;watch;create;update;patch;delete
```

## One Marker Line = One Rule Block in role.yaml

| Go Marker                                                                    | Meaning               |
| ---------------------------------------------------------------------------- | --------------------- |
| `groups=apps.example.com,resources=simpleapps,verbs=get;list;...`            | CRUD on SimpleApp CRs |
| `groups=apps.example.com,resources=simpleapps/status,verbs=get;update;patch` | Read/modify Status    |
| `groups=apps.example.com,resources=simpleapps/finalizers,verbs=update`       | Modify Finalizers     |
| `groups=apps,resources=deployments,verbs=get;list;...`                       | CRUD on Deployments   |

## The 3 Auto-Generated Files and Their Relationships

| Step | File                               | Kind               | Role                                     |
| ---- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------ | ---------------------------------------- |
| 1    | `config/rbac/service_account.yaml` | ServiceAccount     | Issues the `controller-manager` badge    |
| 2    | `config/rbac/role.yaml`            | ClusterRole        | Permission sheet read from markers       |
| 3    | `config/rbac/role_binding.yaml`    | ClusterRoleBinding | Links steps 1 and 2 together             |

How they connect:

```text
service_account.yaml              role.yaml
  name: controller-manager          name: manager-role
  namespace: system                 rules: [auto-generated from markers]
        |                                  |
        +────── role_binding.yaml ─────────+
                  subjects:
                    name: controller-manager   <-- ServiceAccount
                  roleRef:
                    name: manager-role         <-- ClusterRole
```

This is the RBAC concept from Note 05 applied directly:

- Blank badge = `service_account.yaml`
- Permission sheet = `role.yaml`
- Linking badge to permission sheet = `role_binding.yaml`

## Actual File Contents

### service_account.yaml

```yaml
apiVersion: v1
kind: ServiceAccount
metadata:
  name: controller-manager
  namespace: system
```

### role.yaml (auto-generated by make manifests)

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRole
metadata:
  name: manager-role
rules:
  - apiGroups:
      - apps
    resources:
      - deployments
    verbs: ["create", "delete", "get", "list", "patch", "update", "watch"]
  - apiGroups:
      - apps.example.com
    resources:
      - simpleapps
    verbs: ["create", "delete", "get", "list", "patch", "update", "watch"]
  - apiGroups:
      - apps.example.com
    resources:
      - simpleapps/finalizers
    verbs: ["update"]
  - apiGroups:
      - apps.example.com
    resources:
      - simpleapps/status
    verbs: ["get", "patch", "update"]
```

### role_binding.yaml

```yaml
apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1
kind: ClusterRoleBinding
metadata:
  name: manager-rolebinding
roleRef:
  apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io
  kind: ClusterRole
  name: manager-role # <-- name from role.yaml
subjects:
  - kind: ServiceAccount
    name: controller-manager # <-- name from service_account.yaml
    namespace: system
```

## Other Auto-Generated RBAC Files

Managed in `config/rbac/kustomization.yaml`:

| File                                | Purpose                                                 |
| ----------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------- |
| `leader_election_role.yaml`         | Permissions for leader election (only one replica acts)  |
| `leader_election_role_binding.yaml` | Links leader election permissions                       |
| `metrics_auth_role.yaml`            | Metrics endpoint authentication                         |
| `metrics_auth_role_binding.yaml`    | Links metrics authentication permissions                |
| `metrics_reader_role.yaml`          | Metrics read permissions                                |
| `simpleapp_admin_role.yaml`         | Full SimpleApp permissions for cluster administrators   |
| `simpleapp_editor_role.yaml`        | SimpleApp read/write permissions for editors            |
| `simpleapp_viewer_role.yaml`        | SimpleApp read-only permissions for viewers             |

The admin/editor/viewer roles are not used by the Operator itself.
They are convenience Roles intended to be granted to kubectl users.

## Summary: Steps When New Permissions Are Needed

```text
1. Add a marker comment in Go code
   // +kubebuilder:rbac:groups="",resources=configmaps,verbs=get;list

2. Run make manifests
   --> A new rule is automatically added to role.yaml

3. Run make deploy (or make install && make run)
   --> The new permissions are applied to the cluster
```

Never edit the YAML directly. The Go markers are always the source of truth.

## What Each Rule in role.yaml Means

Each rule corresponds 1:1 with what the Reconcile function does:

| Rule (apiGroups / resources) | Meaning | Where It Is Used in Reconcile |
| ---------------------------- | ------- | ----------------------------- |
| `apps` / `deployments` / all verbs | Query, create, modify, delete Deployments | `r.Get()`, `r.Create()`, `r.Update()` |
| `apps.example.com` / `simpleapps` / all verbs | Read/write SimpleApp CRs | `r.Get()` (CR retrieval) |
| `apps.example.com` / `simpleapps/finalizers` / update | Modify the Finalizer field | Not used yet, but pre-generated by kubebuilder |
| `apps.example.com` / `simpleapps/status` / get,patch,update | Modify the Status subresource | Not used yet, but pre-generated by kubebuilder |

## Does It Always Have to Look Like This?

No. It should vary depending on what the Operator does.

Examples:

| What the Operator Does | Additional Permissions Needed |
| ---------------------- | ---------------------------- |
| Read ConfigMaps for configuration | `""` / `configmaps` / get,list,watch |
| Create Services | `""` / `services` / get,create,update,delete |
| Directly delete Pods | `""` / `pods` / get,delete |
| Query Node information | `""` / `nodes` / get,list |

(`""` is the core API group. See 05-rbac.md for a detailed explanation.)

Unnecessary permissions should be removed. This is the Principle of Least Privilege.

## Adding or Modifying Permissions via Markers

### Marker Syntax

```text
// +kubebuilder:rbac:groups=<API group>,resources=<resource>,verbs=<actions>
```

| Field | Value | Example |
| ----- | ----- | ------- |
| groups | API group. Use `""` for core | `""`, `apps`, `apps.example.com` |
| resources | Resource name (plural) | `pods`, `deployments`, `configmaps` |
| verbs | Separated by semicolons (`;`) | `get;list;watch` |
| namespace | (Optional) Include for Role, omit for ClusterRole | `namespace=default` |

### Example: Adding ConfigMap Read Permission

Add one marker line in Go code:

```go
// Existing markers...
// +kubebuilder:rbac:groups=apps,resources=deployments,verbs=get;list;watch;create;update;patch;delete
// +kubebuilder:rbac:groups="",resources=configmaps,verbs=get;list;watch
//                         ^ core API group is an empty string ("")
```

Running `make manifests` automatically adds to `role.yaml`:

```yaml
rules:
  # ...existing rules...
  - apiGroups:
      - ""
    resources:
      - configmaps
    verbs: ["get", "list", "watch"]
```

### Permission Change Workflow

```text
1. Add/modify/delete Go markers
2. make manifests --> role.yaml is automatically regenerated
3. make deploy --> Applied to the cluster
```

## File Generation Timing and Regeneration Behavior

| File                                | Generated When             | Regenerated by `make manifests`?         |
| ----------------------------------- | -------------------------- | ---------------------------------------- |
| `service_account.yaml`              | `kubebuilder init`         | No. Can be edited manually               |
| `role_binding.yaml`                 | `kubebuilder init`         | No. Can be edited manually               |
| `leader_election_role.yaml`         | `kubebuilder init`         | No                                       |
| `leader_election_role_binding.yaml` | `kubebuilder init`         | No                                       |
| `role.yaml`                         | `make manifests`           | Overwritten from Go markers every time   |
| `simpleapp_admin_role.yaml`         | `kubebuilder create api`   | No                                       |
| `simpleapp_editor_role.yaml`        | `kubebuilder create api`   | No                                       |
| `simpleapp_viewer_role.yaml`        | `kubebuilder create api`   | No                                       |

Only `role.yaml` is regenerated each time `make manifests` runs.
The rest are created once during scaffolding and can be edited manually if needed.

## What If You Want to Rename controller-manager?

The name `controller-manager` appears across multiple files:

| File                                            | Part to Change                              |
| ----------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------- |
| `config/rbac/service_account.yaml`              | `name: controller-manager`                  |
| `config/rbac/role_binding.yaml`                 | `subjects.name: controller-manager`         |
| `config/rbac/leader_election_role_binding.yaml` | `subjects.name: controller-manager`         |
| `config/rbac/metrics_auth_role_binding.yaml`    | `subjects.name: controller-manager`         |
| `config/manager/manager.yaml`                   | `serviceAccountName: controller-manager`    |
| `config/manager/manager.yaml`                   | `control-plane: controller-manager` (label) |

Files that reference the label `control-plane: controller-manager` must also be updated:

| File                                               | Reason                         |
| -------------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------ |
| `config/prometheus/monitor.yaml`                   | Metrics collection label selector |
| `config/network-policy/allow-metrics-traffic.yaml` | Network policy label selector  |
| `config/default/metrics_service.yaml`              | Service label selector         |

If even one is missed, permissions may break or metrics collection may stop working.
Unless there is a specific reason, it is safest to keep the names kubebuilder generated.

## Preventing Name Collisions with Multiple Operators

When deploying multiple Operators to the same cluster, it might seem like `controller-manager` would collide.
kubebuilder solves this problem using kustomize.

The two key lines in `config/default/kustomization.yaml`:

```yaml
namespace: myoperator-system # Isolate all resources into this namespace
namePrefix: myoperator-      # Add this prefix to all resource names
```

After `kustomize build`, names are automatically transformed:

| Original Name (YAML) | When deploying myoperator          | When deploying youroperator          |
| --------------------- | ---------------------------------- | ------------------------------------ |
| namespace             | `myoperator-system`                | `youroperator-system`                |
| ServiceAccount        | `myoperator-controller-manager`    | `youroperator-controller-manager`    |
| ClusterRole           | `myoperator-manager-role`          | `youroperator-manager-role`          |
| ClusterRoleBinding    | `myoperator-manager-rolebinding`   | `youroperator-manager-rolebinding`   |

The YAML files use short names (`controller-manager`),
but when actually deployed to the cluster, the prefix + namespace provide isolation and prevent collisions.

This is one of the reasons kubebuilder uses kustomize.

### What If Multiple Operators Are Deployed to the Same Namespace?

As long as the namePrefix is different, there are no collisions even within the same namespace.

```text
Within the myoperator-system namespace:
  myoperator-controller-manager      (ServiceAccount)
  youroperator-controller-manager    (ServiceAccount)
  --> Different names, no problem
```

ClusterRole/ClusterRoleBinding are not namespace-scoped resources -- only one of each name can exist cluster-wide.
However, thanks to namePrefix, the names differ.
Additionally, RBAC is **additive** -- each ClusterRole is bound only to its own ServiceAccount, so they do not interfere with each other.
(There are no rules that "revoke" permissions; permissions are always "added.")

| Scenario                                  | Collision? | Reason                                  |
| ----------------------------------------- | ---------- | --------------------------------------- |
| Same namespace, different namePrefix      | None       | Names are different                     |
| ClusterRole permission scopes overlap     | None       | Each is bound only to its own ServiceAccount |
| Two Operators with the same namePrefix    | Yes        | Names become completely identical       |

Only the last case requires caution.
In practice, if project names are different, the namePrefix will also be different, so this almost never happens.

## Next Step

We will learn the pattern of using the Status subresource to display the current state of a CR.
